articles.title: Forcing Prosecutors To Bring Cases To Keep Their Jobs? What Could Possibly Go Wrong? — See Also
articles.content:
司法部有一个“绝妙”新方法来打击留任律师的士气：强制案件配额。

阅读判决意见……不是那样读：艾米·康尼·巴雷特告诉国会，最高法院并不真的在乎总是解释自己的判决。

法学院不必成为债务陷阱：这些学校在帮助学生支付学费方面做得最好。

老生常谈：保罗·韦斯再失明星诉讼律师。

人工智能不是万能按钮：Sox-ton创始人洛根·布朗解释道，运营真正以AI为核心的律所的日常工作是将工作重心转向流程建设。

这篇文章《强迫检察官为保住工作而起诉案件？会有什么后果？——另见》最初发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
DOJ Has A Cool New Way To Demoralize The Attorneys It Has Left Working For It: Mandatory case quotas.

Read The Opinion… No, Not Like That: Amy Coney Barrett tells Congress that the Supreme Court doesn’t really care about explaining itself all the time.

Law School Doesn’t Need To Be A Debt Sentence: These schools are the best at helping their students with tuition costs.

Dog Bites Man: Paul Weiss loses more star litigators.

AI Is Not The Easy Button: Soxton founder Logan Brown explains that the day-to-day process of running a true AI-first firm shifts the work to building processes.

The post Forcing Prosecutors To Bring Cases To Keep Their Jobs? What Could Possibly Go Wrong? — See Also appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: Has A Law School Had A Dean Facing Court Sanctions Before? Asking For THIS Law School.
articles.content:
昨天，一位联邦法官根据第11条规则及法院固有权力，对丹尼尔·爱泼斯坦实施制裁，原因是他参与特朗普起诉国税局的诉讼——这是一个精心设计的骗局，旨在让特朗普实际上控制“诉”字的两端，并让自己获得和解。爱泼斯坦是特朗普的私人律师，也是斯蒂芬·米勒旗下“美国优先法律”这个反觉醒追责机构的资深成员。下个月，他将成为哪所法学院的临时院长？

提示：至于标题中的附加问题……如果你想到的是约翰·伊士曼，前查普曼法学院院长，他实际上在1月6日事件导致其被吊销律师资格之前数年就已离职。

答案见下一页。

这篇文章首先发表于《Above the Law》。
articles.summary:
Yesterday, a federal judge sanctioned Daniel Epstein under both Rule 11 and the court’s inherent authority for his participation in Trump’s lawsuit against the IRS — a contrivance designed to allow Trump to effectively control both sides of the “v.” and reward himself with a settlement. Epstein, Trump’s personal lawyer and a veteran of Stephen Miller’s wokeness ambulance chaser outfit America First Legal, is set to become the interim dean of which law school next month?

Hint: As for the bonus question in the headline… if you’re thinking of John Eastman, formerly dean of Chapman Law, he had actually left that post years before the January 6 shenanigans that led to his disbarment.

See the answer on the next page.

The post Has A Law School Had A Dean Facing Court Sanctions Before? Asking For THIS Law School. appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: Amy Coney Barrett Then: ‘Read The Opinion!’ ACB Now: Inappropriate To Expect Supreme Court To Explain Decisions
articles.content:
今天，最高法院派埃琳娜·卡根大法官和艾米·康尼·巴雷特大法官前往国会山，就司法部门请求大幅增加安保经费作证。针对联邦法官的威胁——主要是那些裁定反对特朗普政府的下级法院法官——已有充分记录，联邦司法部门寻求约10%的额外资金来应对这些威胁。不过说实话，如果他们坦诚的话，预算增加不仅仅是为了法官的人身安全，还包括改善法院糟糕透顶的网络安全。但强调保护法官免受暴力极端分子侵害要容易得多，而不是指出PACER系统有多烂。

考虑到最高法院大法官并非每天都在国会作证——部分原因是首席大法官罗伯茨认为在公众面前作证是违反三权分立原则的——此次听证会涉及了大法官们很少需要谈论的众多话题。包括臭名昭著的影子议程。

在这个话题上，法院问责组织的亚历克斯·阿罗诺夫捕捉到了巴雷特证词中的关键一点：

2022年，巴雷特大法官斥责公众“去读判决书”。在罗纳德·里根图书馆发表演讲时，她对那些指责最高法院强加赤裸裸政策偏好的人嗤之以鼻，称阅读法院经过深思熟虑的意见会阐明他们对中性适用法治的坚定承诺。如今，她对想要了解最高法院推理的美国公民的立场是：

天哪，她简直从头到尾都在说些言不由衷的废话！

对于不熟悉的人来说，影子议程是最高法院越来越多地在没有辩论或书面解释的情况下发布越来越宽泛命令的非正式称呼。这是一种策略，我们最近了解到它源于首席大法官希望在缺乏法律依据的情况下削弱奥巴马时代环境法规的愿望，此后已经扩散成杰克逊大法官所描述的“卡尔文球”——《卡尔文与霍布斯》漫画中规则不断变化的虚构游戏。

当巴雷特试图用她的“去读判决书”演讲糊弄全国时，她补充道：“这份判决读起来像是纯粹由结果驱动、旨在强加大多数人的政策偏好，还是它实际上是一个诚实的、有说服力的努力——即使你最终不同意——来确定宪法和先例的要求？”她完全清楚，法院已经在堕胎、疫苗接种、投票权等一系列问题上大量依赖未经解释的影子议程命令。

四年过去了，无数未经签署的紧急命令之后，功夫全丢了，现在的说辞变成了“你怎么敢质疑我们？”

公平地说，巴雷特大法官有一个理由解释为什么法院历来很少解释其影子议程命令。当影子议程更准确地被称为“紧急议程”时，这些决定不过是光荣的临时禁令。在一个没有人提交简报的案件上写太多东西，在一个没有人构建的记录上写太多东西，法院可能会不小心对其对案件实质问题的看法发出过早且不恰当的信号。正如一位大法官最近在谈到这一做法时写道，它旨在平衡“说得太多而陷入锁定风险与说得太少而缺乏透明度的成本。”

这正是巴雷特大法官顺便说的。

但这个论点经不起现实检验。避免锁定风险只有在所有人都同意这些命令仅对案件当事人具有约束力、存在唯一目的是维持现状时才有意义。戈萨奇大法官特意让我们明白情况并非如此。在一份附带意见中，戈萨奇咆哮道，法官们“有时可能不同意本法院的判决，但他们永远无权违抗这些判决，”指的是下级法院法官继续遵循现有判例法，而不是从一行字的影子议程命令的字里行间凭感觉判断新先例。作为对影子议程权力这种激进扩张的回应，杨法官道歉了——虽然“被动攻击式地回嘴”可能更准确——他在记录中表示，他“根本不理解紧急议程上的命令属于先例。”

当然，没有人以前理解过，因为它们从来就不是先例。

巴雷特的共和党多数派选择在影子议程的掩护下审理的案件进一步削弱了“这只是临时命令”的说法。多数派并没有寻求维持现状，而是激进地使用影子命令来批准特朗普政府的行动，推翻了多年——实际上是数十年——的现有先例。正如法院在本任期反复裁定的那样，特朗普政府无法随心所欲地做任何事的危害是一种无法弥补的损害，即使它正在采取前所未有的行动。

多数派的影子议程策略，用巴雷特过去的话说，是“纯粹由结果驱动、旨在强加大多数人的政策偏好”，而无需付出“诚实的、有说服力的努力”。部分目的是让特朗普政府获得行动自由，同时保留实际的先例，这样他们日后可以转身告诉未来的民主党政府这些只是临时命令。

但更根本的是，保守派大法官们厌倦了问责。当他们拒绝解释自己的行为意外地留下了一个异议可以利用的漏洞时，他们集体发飙了。塞缪尔·阿利托大法官打破礼仪，从法官席上抱怨几周前索托马约尔大法官拆解了他的意见。而巴雷特大法官在她的回忆录中抱怨她不得不在度假时为多布斯案判决向姐夫辩护。在压倒性的法律先例面前为你的政治优先事项辩护是困难的。他们不喜欢。

恕我直言《爱情故事》：终身任期意味着永远不必说出你的推理理由。

但是，正如阿罗诺夫所指出的，“推理是全部要点。”迈克尔·卢蒂格法官——如果有的话，他是一个保守派中的保守派——在法治协会的会议上表达了完全相同的观点：

在我们的制度和政府中，最高法院根本没有权力，除了通过其关于宪法法律的理性意见而来的权力。每当最高法院在完全没有法律意见的情况下行事——更不用说理性法律意见的情况下——它就是在非法行事，就是这样。它没有钱袋子的权力。它没有剑的权力。它拥有的唯一权力，以及它代表美国人民行使的唯一权力，是其说服力的权力。

南希·格特纳法官说得更直白，解释说影子议程“具有餐巾纸上手写便条的所有正式性”。

这就让我们回到了卡根和巴雷特最初来国会寻求的安全资金问题。最高法院坚决拒绝解释其判决，同时又概要性地推翻反对特朗普政府的下级法院法官的裁决，这促使12名法官将暴力威胁司法机构的责任完全归咎于最高法院。每次最高法院推翻明确先例而不屑于解释自己时，他们就让下级法院法官看起来像是在“针对特朗普”。虽然暴力并非政治光谱某一端的专利，但冲击国会大厦、想要绞死迈克·彭斯的那帮人比在SUV上贴和平标志的足球妈妈更可能升级威胁。

法官面临的威胁是真实存在的。巴雷特大法官和她的同道中人让情况变得更糟，因为他们更关心的是强加其多数派的政策偏好。但也许他们会得到一些额外资金来增加联邦法警。这比不得不解释自己要容易得多。

早期报道：最高法院的影子议程骗局与现实相撞

地区法官奋力拯救法治而司法部和最高法院窃笑

最高法院的影子议程骗局与现实相撞

最高法院刚刚写了“带有转折的卡尔文球法学”，杰克逊大法官写道

最高法院致下级法院：忽略实际的约束性先例，遵循我们未经解释的影子议程感觉

艾米·康尼·巴雷特被迫在家庭旅行中讨论摧毁宪法先例

影子议程备忘录是确凿的证据。所以理所当然地，右翼正在谈论泄密事件。

乔·帕特里斯是Above the Law的高级编辑，也是《像律师一样思考》播客的联合主持人。如有任何提示、问题或评论，请随时发送电子邮件。如果您对法律、政治和适量的大学体育新闻感兴趣，请在Twitter或Bluesky上关注他。
articles.summary:
Today, the Supreme Court sent Justices Elena Kagan and Amy Coney Barrett up Capitol Hill to testify about the judiciary’s request for a substantial increase in security funding. The threats against federal judges — primarily lower court judges ruling against the Trump administration — are well-documented and the federal judiciary seeks approximately 10 percent more funding to address the threat. Though, if they’re being honest, the budget increase isn’t just for the personal security of the judges, as it also covers enhancements to the courts’ abysmal cybersecurity. But it’s a lot easier sell to focus on protecting judges from violent extremists than to point out that PACER sucks.

Given that Supreme Court justices don’t appear before Congress every day — in part because Chief Justice Roberts takes the position that testifying before the public is a separation of powers violation — the hearing touched on a number of topics that the justices rarely have to talk about. Including the infamous shadow docket.

On that subject, Alex Aronson of Court Accountability flagged this key nugget of Barrett’s testimony:

In 2022, Justice Barrett scolded the public to “read the opinion.” Speaking at the Ronald Reagan library, she tut-tutted those who would accuse the Supreme Court of imposing its naked policy preferences that reading the Court’s considered opinions would illuminate their deep and abiding commitment to the neutral application of the rule of law. Today, her stance on citizens who want to understand the Supreme Court’s reasoning is:

Golly, it’s almost like she was spouting disingenuous bullshit the whole time!

For the uninitiated, the shadow docket is the informal name of the Supreme Court’s practice of issuing increasingly expansive orders without argument or written explanation. It’s a tactic that we’ve recently learned grew out of the Chief’s desire to gut Obama-era environmental regulations despite lacking a legal justification, and has since metastasized into what Justice Jackson described as Calvinball — the fictional game from comic strip Calvin & Hobbes where the rules constantly change.

When Barrett tried to gaslight the country with her “read the opinion” speech, she added “[d]oes [the decision] read like something that was purely results driven and designed to impose the policy preferences of the majority, or does this read like it actually is an honest effort and persuasive effort, even if one you ultimately don’t agree with, to determine what the Constitution and precedent requires?” fully aware that the Court was already heavily leaning on unexplained shadow docket orders on issues ranging from abortion to vaccinations to voting rights.

Four years and scores of unsigned emergency orders later, the work is gone and the pitch is how dare you question us?

In fairness to Justice Barrett, there is a reason why the Court historically did little to explain its shadow docket orders. When the use of the shadow docket more accurately fit the title “emergency docket,” these decisions amounted to glorified preliminary injunctions. Write too much about a case that nobody briefed, with a record nobody built, and the Court could accidentally send a premature and improper signal about its sense of the merits. As one justice recently wrote about the practice, it’s designed to strike a balance between “the lock-in risk of saying too much against the transparency cost of saying too little.”

That was Justice Barrett incidentally.

But that argument doesn’t withstand contact with reality. Avoiding a lock-in risk only matters if everyone agrees that these orders aren’t binding beyond the parties in the case and exist solely to maintain the status quo. Justice Gorsuch went out of his way to disabuse us of that notion. In a separate opinion, Gorsuch growled that judges “may sometimes disagree with this Court’s decisions, but they are never free to defy them,” referring to lower court judges continuing to follow existing caselaw rather than vibe check new precedent from between the lines of one-sentence shadow docket orders. In response to this aggressive expansion of shadow docket authority, Judge William Young apologized — though “passive aggressively quipped” is probably more accurate — on the record that he “simply did not understand that orders on the emergency docket were precedent.”

Which, of course, no one did because they never were before.

The cases that Barrett’s Republican majority chose to decide under the cloak of the shadow docket further undermine this it’s just an interim order canard. Rather than seek to maintain the status quo, the majority aggressively used shadow orders to bless Trump administration actions upending years — indeed, decades — of existing precedent. As the Court repeatedly ruled this Term, the harm of the Trump administration not getting to do whatever it wants, whenever it wants is an irreparable harm even if it’s taking unprecedented action.

The majority’s shadow docket strategy is designed, to borrow from Barrett’s past remarks, to be “purely results driven and designed to impose the policy preferences of the majority” without having to put in “an honest effort and persuasive effort.” Part of it is a cynical ploy to give the Trump administration freedom of action while preserving actual precedent so they can turn around and tell a future Democratic administration that these were mere interim orders.

But more fundamentally, the conservative justices are sick and tired of accountability. They threw a collective fit when their refusal to explain themselves accidentally left a loophole that the dissent jumped on. Sam Alito broke decorum to complain from the bench when Justice Sotomayor dismantled his opinion a couple weeks ago. And Justice Barrett bemoaned in her memoir that she had to spend her vacation defending the Dobbs decision to her brother-in-law. Defending your political priorities in the face of overwhelming legal precedent is hard. They don’t like it.

With apologies to Love Story: life tenure means never having to say your reasoning.

But, as Aronson notes, “the reasoning is the whole point.” Judge Michael Luttig — a conservative’s conservative if there ever was one — made the exact same point at the Society for the Rule of Law conference:

The Supreme Court has no power at all in our system and government, except that power that comes to it by virtue of [its] reasoned opinions of constitutional law. Whenever the Supreme Court is acting without opinions of law — at all — let alone, reasoned opinions of law. It is acting illegitimately, period. It doesn’t have the power of the purse. It doesn’t have the power of the sword. The only power it has, and the only power that it has to wield on behalf of the American people is the power of its persuasion.

Judge Nancy Gertner put it even more bluntly, explaining that the shadow docket has “all the formality of notes on a napkin.”

Which brings us all the way back around to the security funds that Kagan and Barrett came to Congress for in the first place. The Supreme Court’s steadfast refusal to explain its decisions while summarily overturning lower court judges ruling against the Trump administration inspired 12 judges to place blame squarely on the Supreme Court for its role in driving violent threats against the judiciary. Every time the Supreme Court knocks down crystal clear precedent without deigning to explain themselves, they leave a lower court judge looking like they were just “out to get Trump.” And while violence is hardly the province of one end of the political spectrum, the dudes who stormed the Capitol looking to hang Mike Pence are more likely to escalate their threats than a soccer mom with a peace sign on her SUV.

The threats facing judges are real. Justice Barrett and her fellow travelers have made it worse because they care more about imposing the policy preferences of their majority. But maybe they’ll get a few extra bucks for more federal marshals. A lot easier than having to explain themselves.

Earlier: Supreme Court’s Shadow Docket Scam Collides With Reality
District Judges Fight To Save The Rule Of Law While DOJ And Supreme Court Snicker
Supreme Court’s Shadow Docket Scam Collides With Reality
Supreme Court Just ‘Calvinball Jurisprudence With A Twist,’ Writes Justice Jackson
Supreme Court To Lower Courts: Ignore Actual Binding Precedent, Follow Our Unexplained Shadow Docket Vibes Instead
Amy Coney Barrett Forced To Discuss Destroying Constitutional Precedent During Family Trip
The Shadow Docket Memos Are Damning. So Naturally, The Right Is Talking About The Leak.

Joe Patrice is a senior editor at Above the Law and co-host of Thinking Like A Lawyer. Feel free to email any tips, questions, or comments. Follow him on Twitter or Bluesky if you’re interested in law, politics, and a healthy dose of college sports news.

The post Amy Coney Barrett Then: ‘Read The Opinion!’ ACB Now: Inappropriate To Expect Supreme Court To Explain Decisions appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: Geoffrey Brow On Why the Best In-House Lawyers Learn To Fly The Plane With One Engine Burning
articles.content:
大多数律师接受的训练都是规避不确定性。

但也有像杰弗里·布劳（Geoffrey Brow）这样的律师，他花了多年时间帮助企业构建全新技术，这些技术太新了，以至于没有法律手册可循，没有先例可援引，有时甚至没有监管者完全理解正在构建的东西。

这改变了律师的工作。

布劳在维珍超级高铁（Virgin Hyperloop）工作时，他不是在谈判那些套着更新术语的熟悉商业模式，而是在围绕一种通过真空管以每小时数百英里速度运行的交通系统来构建法律关系。在此之前，他曾在谷歌从事无人机配送系统的咨询工作。如今，作为Roku公司的助理总法律顾问，他横跨运营、工程、产品、人工智能、开源软件以及持续快速演变的各类技术交易领域，而这些交易的发展速度往往超过周围法律框架的跟进速度。

贯穿其中的一条主线不是技术本身，而是在不确定性中做出判断。

这就是这场对话变得有趣的地方。

**律师作为建设者，而非审查者**

关于交易法律工作最大的误解之一是，它实际上是打着专业旗号的程序性工作。布劳的看法截然不同。在他看来，合同不是文书工作，而是运营架构。

“商业交易合同实际上只是交易的描述，”他解释道。“它包含所有相关事项。你什么时候交付？出了问题怎么办？你必须填补所有空白。”

这种框架之所以重要，是因为它改变了你对法律实践的思考方式。律师不是站在业务外部举红牌的人。律师身处系统内部，努力将模糊性转化为可执行的结构。

布劳在我们的讨论中反复回到创造力这个话题，这个词大多数律师不会本能地与合同联系起来。然而他的职业生涯清楚地证明了这一点。当你处理的是从未存在过的技术时，法律起草就不再是循例练习，而变成了应用设计。

“没有任何先例。没有判例法。完全是全新的，”他在描述超级高铁工作时说道。“这要求你发挥创造力，思考这些交易的风险和回报，以及如何最好地为公司客户服务。”

这种创造力不会在孤立中产生。它需要与工程师、运营人员和产品团队深度融合。布劳描述了与工程师们进行的长谈，目的是不仅理解技术应该做什么，还要理解它可能在哪里失败，哪些假设是不确定的，以及公司本身在哪里还没有完全找到答案。

“需要与业务团队和工程团队进行大量提问，才能弄清楚他们想要做什么、存在哪些风险、如何减轻这些风险，然后将其记录下来。”

这就是现代内部法律实践的一句话概括。

**内部律师为何必须忘却完美**

我们讨论中最精彩的部分之一集中在从精英律所培训到内部实践的转型上。

布劳在进入技术交易领域之前，曾在纽约和伦敦从事传统公司业务多年。他对大所培训的评价带着明显的敬意。严谨很重要。精确很重要。高强度也很重要。

但最终，其中一些本能变成了障碍。

“在大型律所环境中，表现水平必须达到百分之百，”他说。“发出的文件不能有拼写错误，不能有任何错误。必须绝对完美。”

然后他进入了企业内部。

“你必须认识到，你客户的主要产业不是法律，”他解释道。“他们想要的是完成交易。”

这种转变听起来很明显，直到你亲身体验。许多律师从未在心理上完全完成这种转型。他们继续优化技术完美，而此时业务已经转向运营执行。

布劳描述了他看到律师们因为不完美的格式、混乱的谈判或未解决的起草细节而纠结的场景，因为他们被训练成相信每一个松散的线头都代表着失败。

内部工作迫使你采用不同的优先级模型。

“你必须认识到核心问题是什么，”他说。“哪些是最重要的风险敞口或需要实现的目标？”

这种思维方式在技术公司尤为重要，因为在技术公司，速度本身成为竞争环境的一部分。法务部门不能表现得像脱离运营现实的外部审查委员会。它必须作为决策系统的一部分来运作。

这并不意味着放弃严谨。这意味着学会比例原则。

**飞机部件脱落时依然飞行**

布劳曾用一个比喻完美捕捉了精益技术环境中内部法律工作的情感现实。

“我想到一架正在飞行的飞机，”他说。“各种各样的部件开始从飞机上脱落。螺栓、金属板，诸如此类。你的任务是把飞机降落。”

然后他停顿了一下。

“飞机降落时可能看起来很糟糕，但你让所有人活了下来。”

这可能是我很长时间以来听到的关于内部实践最诚实的描述。

律所通常围绕完美进行优化。内部团队围绕连续性进行优化。目标不是理论上的优雅。目标是让组织在不完美的条件下持续向前推进。

而且越来越多的现实是，这意味着在法律框架远远落后于技术现实的环境中做出决策。

布劳在企业交易和消费者面向系统之间划出了一个重要的区别。在B2B环境中，老练的各方通常明知接受更高程度的不确定性和实验性。消费者面向产品则不同。下游后果会变得更加严重。

“当产品直接影响消费者时，你不能真正承担太多风险，”他说。

在人工智能时代，这种区别比以往任何时候都更加重要。

**人工智能将继续改变这份工作。判断力仍然最重要。**

在我们讨论接近尾声时，我们转向了年轻律师在人工智能重塑法律工作时应该关注什么。

布劳的回答值得注意，因为它不以技术为中心。

它以培训为中心。

“我会鼓励任何从法学院毕业的人选择一条能够获得真正指导和学习机会的道路，”他说。

他坦率地谈到了困难环境的价值，尤其是在职业生涯早期。不是因为苦难本身有价值，而是因为重复、观察和接触会培养出在其他地方难以复制的直觉。

“你会学会如何起草。你会看到合伙人在电话会议上的表现，他们如何谈判，”他说。“这是一个真正的熔炉。”

然后他说了一些许多律师忘记的事情。

“尽可能多地写作。”

不是因为律师需要更优美的文字。而是因为起草就是结构化思考。合同是通过语言表达的操作逻辑。如果律师失去了通过写作清晰思考的能力，他们就失去了法律判断实际上得以发挥的核心机制之一。

这一观察此刻感觉格外贴切。人工智能可以加速起草。它可以总结。它可以综合。但律师仍然必须决定什么重要、风险在哪里、哪些权衡是可以接受的，以及不确定性应该如何分配。

技术变了。责任没有。

**那些茁壮成长的律师保持好奇**

布劳在最后提出的一个观点在我结束对话后仍然萦绕心头。

“保持开放的心态，”他说。“你可能会发现一个你之前从未考虑过的法律领域。”

他自己的职业生涯反映了这一现实。他上法学院时并没有打算从事超级高铁系统、无人机配送框架或人工智能赋能产品环境的工作。他追随好奇心，拥抱陌生的领域，一次又一次地允许自己进化。

对于当下的内部律师来说，这可能是更深层的教训。

未来可能不属于记住最多规则的律师。它将属于那些能够进入陌生系统、提出更好的问题、理解技术实际如何运作、以及在尚无明确路线图时帮助组织负责任地向前推进的律师。

这需要技术技能。

也需要对不确定性的适应能力。

也许还需要偶尔愿意在只剩一台发动机运转时将飞机降落下来的意愿。

---

**关于作者**

奥尔加·V·麦克（Olga V. Mack）是TermScout的首席执行官，她在那里构建的法律系统使合同更容易理解、更容易操作、在真实商业条件下更值得信赖。她的工作专注于法律规则如何分配权力、管理风险以及在不确定性下塑造决策。作为一名连续创业者兼前法务总监，奥尔加此前领导一家法律科技公司被LexisNexis收购。她在加州大学伯克利法学院任教，也是CodeX斯坦福法律信息学中心的研究员。她著有多部关于法律创新和技术的书籍，发表过六次TEDx演讲，她的见解经常出现在《福布斯》、《彭博法律》、《VentureBeat》、《TechCrunch》和《Above the Law》上。她的工作将法律视为关键基础设施，为组织的实际运作方式而设计。

本文最初发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
Most lawyers are trained to avoid uncertainty.

Then there are lawyers like Geoffrey Brow, who spent years helping companies build technologies so new that there was no legal playbook to follow, no precedent to cite, and sometimes no regulator who fully understood what was being built.

That changes the job.

When Brow worked on Virgin Hyperloop, he was not negotiating familiar commercial patterns wrapped in updated terminology. He was helping structure legal relationships around a transportation system moving hundreds of miles per hour through vacuum tubes. Before that, he advised on drone delivery systems at Google. Today, as assistant general counsel at Roku, he works across operations, engineering, products, AI, open source software, and technology transactions that continue to evolve faster than the surrounding legal frameworks.

The common thread is not technology. It is judgment under uncertainty.

And that is where this conversation became interesting.

The Lawyer As Builder, Not Reviewer

One of the biggest misconceptions about transactional law is that it is procedural work disguised as expertise. Brow sees it differently. For him, contracts are not paperwork. They are an operational architecture.

“A commercial transaction contract is really just a description of the deal,” he explained. “It’s all the stuff that goes with it. When are you going to deliver the thing? What happens if the thing goes wrong? You have to fill in all the gaps.”

That framing matters because it changes how you think about legal practice. The lawyer is not sitting outside the business throwing flags. The lawyer is inside the system, trying to translate ambiguity into an executable structure.

Brow repeatedly returned to creativity during our discussion, which is not a word most lawyers instinctively associate with contracts. Yet his career makes the point clearly. When you are working on technology that has never existed before, legal drafting stops being a precedent exercise and starts becoming applied design.

“There’s no precedent. There’s no case law. It is completely new,” he said while describing Hyperloop work. “That requires you to be creative and think about the risks and rewards of those transactions and how to best serve your corporate client.”

That creativity does not happen in isolation. It requires deep integration with engineers, operators, and product teams. Brow described long conversations with engineers to understand not only what a technology was supposed to do, but where it could fail, where assumptions were uncertain, and where the company itself did not yet fully know the answers.

“It takes a lot of questioning with business and engineering to figure out what they want to do, what the risks are, how you can mitigate those risks, and then document it.”

That is modern in-house practice in one sentence.

Why In-House Lawyers Have To Unlearn Perfection

One of the strongest parts of our discussion focused on the transition from elite law firm training into in-house practice.

Brow spent years in New York and London doing traditional corporate work before moving into technology transactions. He described the training at large firms with obvious respect. The rigor mattered. The precision mattered. The intensity mattered.

But eventually, some of those instincts became obstacles.

“In the Biglaw environment, the performance level has to be a thousand percent,” he said. “Nothing goes out with a typo. Nothing is incorrect. It has to be absolutely perfect.”

Then he went in-house.

“What you have to realize is that the primary industry of your client is not law,” he explained. “They want transactions done.”

That shift sounds obvious until you live it. Many lawyers never fully make the transition psychologically. They continue optimizing for technical perfection long after the business has moved on to operational execution.

Brow described watching lawyers struggle with imperfect formatting, messy negotiations, or unresolved drafting nuances because they were trained to believe every loose thread represented failure.

In-house work forces a different prioritization model.

“You have to realize what the core issues are,” he said. “What are the most important vulnerabilities or goals that need to be achieved?”

That mindset is especially critical in technology companies where speed itself becomes part of the competitive environment. The legal department cannot behave like an external review committee detached from operational realities. It has to function as part of the decision-making system.

That does not mean abandoning rigor. It means learning proportionality.

Flying The Plane While Parts Fall Off

At one point, Brow offered an analogy that perfectly captured the emotional reality of in-house legal work inside lean technology environments.

“I think about an airplane flying along,” he said. “All sorts of parts start to fall off the airplane. Bolts, sheets of metal, what have you. Your job is to get the plane on the ground.”

Then he paused.

“The plane might look really ugly when you land, but you’ve kept everyone alive.”

That is probably the most honest description of in-house practice I have heard in a long time.

Law firms are often optimized around perfection. In-house teams are optimized around continuity. The goal is not theoretical elegance. The goal is sustainable forward movement under imperfect conditions.

And increasingly, that means making decisions in environments where legal frameworks lag far behind technological reality.

Brow drew an important distinction between enterprise transactions and consumer-facing systems. In B2B contexts, sophisticated parties often knowingly accept higher levels of uncertainty and experimentation. Consumer-facing products are different. The downstream consequences become materially more serious.

“You can’t really take too much risk” when products affect consumers directly, he said.

That distinction matters more now than ever in the age of AI.

AI Will Keep Changing The Job. Judgment Still Matters Most.

Toward the end of our discussion, we shifted toward what younger lawyers should focus on as AI reshapes legal work.

Brow’s answer was notable because it was not centered on technology.

It was centered on training.

“I would encourage anyone graduating from law school to choose a path where they are going to get true mentorship and learning opportunities,” he said.

He spoke candidly about the value of difficult environments, especially early in a career. Not because suffering is inherently valuable, but because repetition, observation, and exposure build instincts that cannot easily be replicated elsewhere.

“You’ll learn how to draft. You’ll see how partners act on conference calls, how they negotiate,” he said. “It is a true crucible.”

Then he said something many lawyers forget.

“Write as much as you can.”

Not because lawyers need prettier prose. Because drafting is structured thinking. Contracts are operational logic expressed through language. If lawyers lose the ability to think clearly through writing, they lose one of the core mechanisms through which legal judgment actually gets exercised.

That observation feels especially relevant right now. AI can accelerate drafting. It can summarize. It can synthesize. But lawyers still have to decide what matters, where risk sits, what tradeoffs are acceptable, and how uncertainty should be allocated.

The technology changes. The responsibility does not.

The Lawyers Who Thrive Stay Curious

One of the final points Brow made stayed with me after the conversation ended.

“Keep an open mind,” he said. “You might find an area of law you never even considered before.”

His own career reflects that reality. He did not go to law school intending to work on Hyperloop systems, drone delivery frameworks, or AI-enabled product environments. He followed curiosity, embraced unfamiliar territory, and repeatedly allowed himself to evolve.

That may be the deeper lesson for in-house lawyers right now.

The future probably will not belong to the lawyers who memorize the most rules. It will belong to the lawyers who can enter unfamiliar systems, ask better questions, understand how technology actually works, and help organizations move forward responsibly when no clear roadmap exists yet.

That requires technical skill.

It also requires comfort with uncertainty.

And maybe, occasionally, the willingness to land the plane with one engine on fire.

Olga V. Mack is the CEO of TermScout, where she builds legal systems that make contracts faster to understand, easier to operate, and more trustworthy in real business conditions. Her work focuses on how legal rules allocate power, manage risk, and shape decisions under uncertainty. A serial CEO and former General Counsel, Olga previously led a legal technology company through acquisition by LexisNexis. She teaches at Berkeley Law and is a Fellow at CodeX, the Stanford Center for Legal Informatics. She has authored several books on legal innovation and technology, delivered six TEDx talks, and her insights regularly appear in Forbes, Bloomberg Law, VentureBeat, TechCrunch, and Above the Law. Her work treats law as essential infrastructure, designed for how organizations actually operate.

The post Geoffrey Brow On Why the Best In-House Lawyers Learn To Fly The Plane With One Engine Burning appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: Welcoming The World
articles.content:
随着世界杯最后三场比赛的临近，现在正是回顾过去一个月所发生一切的绝佳时机。在这段时间里，整个世界似乎都来到了美国做客。首先，我们再次见证了美国梦激发世界各地游客的能力——主要体现在我们许多人常常理所当然忽视的那种富足上。从外国球迷对布西(Buc-ee's)便利店的惊叹，到一些人带着牧场沙拉酱和其他美国“奢侈品”回国，能够透过他人的眼光看待我们的国家，有力地提醒了我们许多人的生活有多么美好。其次，我们彻底证明美国观众会收看国际级足球比赛，其收视率仅次于NFL分区决赛和超级碗。（在撰写本文时世界杯决赛尚未播出，如果决赛收视率仅次于超级碗，我不会感到意外。）最后，我们认识到，非常好（美国国家队）与伟大（从比利时水平及以上的每支球队）之间的差距仍然是一道鸿沟，就像最盈利的大型律师事务所与其他所有律所之间的差距一样。

距离我上次在这些版面撰写关于重大足球锦标赛的文章已经过去14年了——是的，我从事这一行已经相当长的时间——但毫无疑问，今年的世界杯激发了公众的想象力，也掏空了他们的钱包。从第一场比赛开球之前开始，票价、停车费到周边商品的价格就成为新闻报道的热门话题。尽管如此，从墨西哥城阿兹特克体育场那片缺氧的熔炉般的赛场，到美国各地那些缺乏赞助商的NFL大教堂——这些绿茵场一直是场上戏剧的舞台——比赛在美国各地售罄。作为一个长期的足球迷，也作为一个足球迷的父亲，我一直设想，一旦世界杯自1994年以来首次来到这片土地，我会至少现场观看一场比赛（如果不止一场的话）。然而，多亏了FIFA混乱的赛前购票系统，更确切地说是由于即便是最不吸引人的小组赛价格也高得离谱，我在锦标赛开始时没有买到任何比赛的门票——而且对此感到满意。尽管如此，总有一种挥之不去的感觉，觉得在本土参加现场世界杯的机会转瞬即逝，最好抓住机会。

幸运的是，我的大儿子继续推动我考虑去看比赛。当摩洛哥和法国之间的四分之一决赛确定在波士顿一个周四下午进行时，一次短途公路旅行的基本框架开始成型。我们可以周三下午晚些时候出发，在体育场附近的一家免费（积分）酒店住周三晚上，周四看比赛，然后周四晚上开车回家。这计划很合理，虽然体育场附近的酒店剩余房间的价格已经达到了暴风雪后铲雪费般的敲诈水平，但在五英里以内有免费选项，可以用积分预订。唯一的问题是，我们没有门票，直接从FIFA购买的价格相当于往返巴黎的商务舱机票费用。二级市场的选择也好不了多少，没有一张门票低于1500美元——而这个价格只能让你坐在波士顿（吉列）体育场上层区域的座位上。加上270美元的停车费——但前提是提前24小时以上预付，之后价格只会更高——参加比赛的成本看起来将超过一趟精打细算的前往法国和摩洛哥的为期一周旅行。

不过，正如那句老话所说：“足球是父亲和儿子的游戏。”所以当我儿子在二级票务网站上以相当于我们观看巴西在无关紧要的小组赛中蹂躏海地的价格，找到了很棒的下层座位时，我决定不要错过这个机会。我很高兴做了这个选择，尽管比赛本身对明显更优秀的法国队来说只是一次轻松的散步。尽管缺乏戏剧性，但看到个人和团队层面展现的技艺水平令人振奋。我能想到的最接近的法律类比是观看一个训练有素的庭审团队呈现一场引人入胜的法庭表演，从首席出庭律师到初级律师助理，每个人都为成功的结果做出贡献。不过，我从这次经历中学到的更大教训是，我们真正生活在一个全球经济中，美国在提供成功机会方面仍然被视为领导者。以我们从酒店到体育场的短途优步(Uber)司机为例，他是一位埃及移民——就像我的父母一样——他将在一个月内在体育场综合体附近开设他的新中东餐厅。当我们愉快地交流各自喜爱的埃及菜肴时，听到他的故事对我是一个有力的提醒，世界各地许多人的心中都跳动着创业精神，包括那些选择来到这片土地抓住机会实现梦想的人。同样，看到人群中——尤其是体育场周围的赛前时刻——的欢乐，从穿着醒目红色球衣的四位挪威人乐于在陌生人召唤时“划船”，到在售票入口外的步行区跳舞的热情摩洛哥球迷——使这成为一次难忘的经历，参加这次活动的费用和努力是完全值得的。

最终，作为律师，我们能亲身体验全球性事件的机会并不多。对我来说，这次与自身身份各个组成部分互动的机会——作为一个第一代美国人，父母出生于埃及，父亲在法国长大——激起了感恩之情，感恩这个国家给予我的一切。当我反思自己参加世界杯比赛作为一次迷你夏季公路旅行的经历时，我感到幸运能够成为参与这一盛事的美国人之一。我们生活在一个小小的世界里的提醒是深刻的，体育可以将表面上可能有天壤之别的人们聚在一起。当我们等待最后的比赛决定最终冠军时，我认为每个美国人都应该为我们如何迎接世界而感到自豪。

如有评论或问题，欢迎发送至gaston@k2k.law或通过Twitter：@gkroub。任何主题建议或想法都热烈欢迎。

加斯顿·克鲁布(Gaston Kroub)居住在布鲁克林，是K2K知识产权律师事务所的创始合伙人，这是一家知识产权诉讼精品律所，同时也作为投资界的专利问题领先咨询机构。加斯顿的业务重点是知识产权诉讼及相关咨询，尤其专注于专利事务。您可以通过gaston@k2k.law或Twitter @gkroub与他联系。

这篇文章《迎接世界》首次发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
As we approach the final three games of the World Cup, it is as good a time as any to reflect on some of the things we have learned from the past month or so, when it seems like the world has come to visit the United States. First, we have been reminded of the capacity of the American Dream to inspire visitors from around the world — mostly in terms of the abundance that many of us so often unfortunately take for granted. From foreign fans marveling at Buc-ee’s, to others returning to their home country with bottles of Ranch dressing and other American “luxuries,” being able to see our country through the eyes of others has been a potent reminder of how good many of us have things. Second, we have established once and for all that an American audience will tune in to watch soccer being played at international level, with ratings that beat out every other broadcast other than the NFL conference championship games and the Super Bowl. (With the World Cup final yet to air as of the time of this writing, I would not be surprised if the final’s ratings are only surpassed by the Super Bowl itself.) Lastly, we have learned that the gap between very good (U.S. national team) and great (every team from Belgium’s level and upward) remains a chasm, much like the separation between the most profitable Biglaw firms and everyone else. 

While it has been 14 years — yes, I have been at this for quite a while — since I last wrote about a major soccer tournament on these pages, there is no doubt that this year’s World Cup has captured the public’s imagination — as well as their dollars, with prices at games for everything from tickets to parking to merchandise serving as newscast fodder from before the first ball was even kicked. Still, games have been sold out across North America, from the oxygen-deprived cauldron of Mexico City’s Azteca to the sponsor-bereft NFL cathedrals across the USA that have been the staging grounds for the onfield drama. As a longtime soccer fan, as well as the father of soccer fans, I had always imagined that once the World Cup came to these shores for the first time since 1994, I would attend at least one game (if not more) live. Thanks, however, to FIFA’s convoluted pretournament ticket procurement system and even more so due to the stratospheric prices for even the most unappealing group-stage matchups, I went into the tournament without a ticket to a single match — and was happy about it. Still, there was a nagging feeling that the opportunity to attend a live World Cup match on home soil was a fleeting one, best acted on.

Thankfully, my eldest son continued to push me to consider going to a match. And when the quarter-final was set between Morocco and France on a late Thursday afternoon in Boston, the rough parameters of a short road trip to take in the action started to formulate. We could leave late afternoon Wednesday, stay at a free (points) hotel near the stadium Wednesday night, attend the match Thursday, and drive home Thursday night. It made sense, and while the hotels in the immediate vicinity of the stadium were selling their remaining rooms at post-blizzard snow shovel price-gouging levels, there were free options less than five miles away with availability on points. The only hitch was that we didn’t have tickets, with the available options direct from FIFA priced at round-trip business class flights to Paris amounts. And the secondary market options were only slightly better, with nary a ticket available for less than $1,500 per seat — with that amount snagging you a seat in the upper reaches of Boston (nee Gillette) Stadium’s environs. Add in the $270 parking charge — but only if you prepaid more than 24 hours before game time, after which it got even more expensive — and the cost of attendance was looking like it would exceed a well-budgeted weeklong jaunt to both France and Morocco proper.

Still, as the old quote goes “football is a game of fathers and sons,” so when my son’s deft and persistent navigation of the secondary ticket sites turned up great lower-level seats for what it would have cost us to see Brazil eviscerate Haiti in a meaningless group-stage game, I decided not to miss out on the experience. I am glad that I made that choice, even if the game itself turned out to be a stroll in the park for the clearly superior French team. Despite the lack of drama, the thrill in seeing the level of skill and talent on display, on both an individual and team level, was exhilarating. The closest legal analog I could think of was watching a well-honed trial team put on a captivating courtroom performance, with input from the lead trial lawyer down to the junior paralegal all contributing to a successful outcome. That said, the bigger lessons that I learned from the experience was the reminder that we truly live and function in a global economy, one where the United States is still looked at as a leader in terms of providing opportunities for success to those courageous enough to seize them. As a case in point, our Uber driver on the short ride from the hotel to the stadium was an Egyptian immigrant, like my parents, who was within a month of opening his new Middle Eastern restaurant in the shadow of the stadium complex. While we enjoyed exchanging notes on our favorite Egyptian dishes, hearing his story was a potent reminder for me that the entrepreneurial spirit beats in many hearts around the world, including in those who choose to come to these shores to seize their chances to put their dreams into action. Likewise, seeing the joy in the crowd, especially pregame around the stadium — from the four Norwegians in their distinctive red kits happy to “row” at the beckoning of strangers to the ebullient Moroccan fans dancing in the pedestrian zone just outside the ticket gates — made it an unforgettable experience, and well worth the cost and effort to attend.

Ultimately, there are not many opportunities we get as lawyers to experience a global phenomenon first hand. For myself, the chance to interact with the various components of my identity, as a first-generation American, with parents born in Egypt and a dad who grew up in France, stirred up feelings of gratitude for everything that this country has given me. As I reflect on my experience attending a World Cup game as part of a mini-summer road trip, I feel fortunate to have had the experience of being one of the Americans who got to participate in the festivities. The reminder that we live in a small world was a potent one, where sport could act to bring together people who on the surface may have stark differences. As we wait for the final matches to determine the ultimate winner, I think every American should feel proud at how we have welcomed the world.
